replies.author: @PelemusMcSoy
replies.text:
When I hosted Star Wars tabletop games, I knew very little about the expanded universe. While I added my own characters and handful of species, I would research the Wookiepedia to learn about planets, species, politics, etc. to ensure my world made sense. Was it the best source? No, but it was the best I had each week, and I made sure to dive as deep as I could for a weekly game.

If I was going to make a full on show, movie, or game, you damn well better believe I'd read the books to get a better understanding to not disrupt existing lore.

replies.text: ​@gemelwalters2942 it's not explicitly his race but Snape has one of the most often and most detailed physical descriptions in the books and it *is* tied in with his character. He is described as having sallow skin, a hooked nose and "curtains of greasy black hair". It is physically impossible for a black person to have sallow skin, very unusual to see a hooked nose on a black person, if ever, and highly unlikely to have "curtains of greasy hair" as in hanging straight and limply. These features are remarked on repeatedly in the books and are directly linked with (perceptions of) his character. If it were an audiobook I would agree that performance strength is all that matters. But to suggest that appearance doesn't matter to a *visual medium* production is straight-up disingenuous, particularly when a character has an extremely well-known and described-in-detail appearance in the source material. I don't know if you're at all familiar with Anne of Green Gables but if you are, it would be akin to casting her as anything other than a red-head.

replies.author: @mindsmoothie
replies.text:
​​​​@gemelwalters2942 The ideology of Voldemort and his followers is very much shaped by real-world history, particularly the Nazis and their obsession with racial purity. The story has a whole world built around the fear of "impurity" which aligns with the very real-world fascist movement, where RACE, ETHNICITY, and "bloodlines" were key ideological tools used by JKR. The core themes of Harry Potter—good vs. evil, inclusivity vs. exclusivity, freedom vs. oppression—don't really delve into racial diversity within the Death Eater ranks, sure—but, having a black character in their ranks would create a significant contradiction world building-wise... They ARE focused on blood purity and the oppression of marginalized groups (like muggle-borns & non-magical people). So, it's not that such a character 'couldn't' be involved, it's just that the story LITERALLY wasn't built with that inclusion in mind... meaning it DOES fundamentally alter the story even still. Not necessarily in "bad" ways but again the creators claimed "accuracy" but it CAN'T be—for example: Lily, a muggle-born, being friends with a child Snape (better?) at a time when the wizarding world was DEEPLY SEGREGATED by BOTH RACE and blood status 'could' in fact make their connection even more revolutionary indeed—BUT—the story has too account for this alteration (well... to be *good* it should... and you're AGAIN making a DIFFERENT/INACCURATE story, even if it's amazing.) I bet the actor will be great in fact! But a change like this cannot be superficial... which you seem to not get... Snape suddenly made a different race that definitely wouldn't exist easily amongst the death-eaters + WITHOUT exploring his deeper complexities with his character, motivations, experiences & yeah... his race too as it would DEFINITELY play a part in that too—would be SHALLOW & 'forced' (the 2nd being what everyone is primarily upset about I think, but idk.) If Snapes' planned story is about being "the guy that looks nothing like the rest of the death-eaters in such a way it doesn't  make sense in their world & is also a double agent," risks reducing his complexity and reducing the nuanced layers that make Snape so fascinating. Of course he's still "just a man" but he's a man in a VERY TENSE world. Snape being a death-eater as it stands in the show = an inherent contradiction in his alignment with such a group [and it MUST BE ADDRESSED—as in: his position to join such a movement + how he reconciles the disparity between his own status and the Death Eaters' ideology. ]. Of course its important to avoid making Snapes' race the sole focus of his character, but changing his identity in this world while focusing on other elements like his psychological complexity, his redemption arc vs his tragic past & not acknowledging the racial dynamics at play = erasing an important part of his potential character development + DOESN’T fit the world building at all [& is NOT accurate]. Also... idk if you know... but he grew up in a racist society... that would affect his character! So they'd REALLY need too explain these things.

Current Snape—considering the segregation of his youth—the story would have to reckon with both class AND racial issues. Which does make his journey even more complex which is intriguing for sure! But it's a stark departure from the original character. The Lily/Snape dynamic currently risks reinforcing a stereotype where characters—especially black men—are often defined by their unrequited love for white characters [which is why I said yikes way earlier btw as I believe their relationship needs too be handled VERY carefully to avoid this]. Snape currently—fascinating idea as a whole—BUT... if you’re trying to create an 'accurate' show based on the Harry Potter books and world-building, it creates a PARADOX. Like... how does this character really rise to become one of Voldemorts' most trusted followers... given that the Death Eaters themselves are driven by a belief in racial superiority??? It HAS TOO be developed to avoid LOGICAL INCONSISTENCIES—it 'demands' an entirely different set of world-building adjustments to make sense. Again a cool idea and to be well done [given the world parameters] the show would need too confront race and prejudice head-on [something that’s barely touched on in the books... 😮‍💨] & doing so would require rewriting major parts of his backstory and the story itself... ideally in such a way that's respectful to the characters' complexity and DOESN’T reduce him to a cliché 🙃...

But AGAIN. replies.author: @Kirk-36Moshpit
replies.text:
​@BofansonDiZnatsI understand; I've been wanting a TV series that could include all the slice-of-life elements and character beats the movies left out since I was a kid - the last thing I want is for the integrity of the story to be sacrificed on the altar of DEI politics.

The possible negative optics of a black actor being cast as Snape in particular - that's something I hadn't considered, and it's worth discussing how that can be solved outside of recasting (although Dean Thomas and Professor Sinistra were both explicitly Black in the books, and Dean was portrayed as a good kid throughout the whole series, so just having him as a contrast to Snape could make the point clear that it's not your skin color but your character and circumstances that determine your path). There's also the fact that they're iffy on including Rowling (controversy or not, she wrote the books) and somehow decided hiring ANOTHER director who hadn't read the books was a good idea.

I'd rather talk about those problems and potential solutions, but most of the people here seem more interested in making derogatory comments about Black people, and that doesn't help matters. Sorry this took so long.

replies.text:
They only remember the last movie where it looks like Snape's a misunderstood antihero who earned peace but was disliked at face value, like what Hollywood thinks all black people go through (because they're projecting their own behaviour onto others).
Book Snape created an improved recipe for the perfect poison that kills with just a single touch of the tiniest drop, and created a spell that lacerates you from top to bottom like a butchered pig just because he was in a bad mood one day. He joined the Death Eaters and tortured and presumably killed people, and didn't play the double agent role until the love of his life was murdered by his master. He overtly despises Harry on every occasion precisely because he's not Harry's father but in his dreams could've been, because Harry reminds him of the bully who won in the end, and even when Snape dies, the tone of his difficult relationship with Harry is "you remind me of pain". He didn't protect Harry because he liked the guy, but because he's still simping for the girl who cucked him 15+ years after she died.

This is not a character that suits political virtue signaling in the 21st century. It's a tragic failure of a wronged fool who for once in his life did a good thing, and it was for sad reasons.

Unfortunately, I think it’s quite common. When Disney made a TV show out of my other favorite book series, the director confidently declared he had never read the books. It showed. It was an insulting, degrading disaster that bore no resemblance to the original source material or its characters. 

The woke casting was really only about 10% of that debacle, but their insistence on casting a disabled actor as the disabled main character meant giving him … the wrong disability. Never mind that the actor looked nothing like the protagonist. They went out of their way to make Tudor England more racially diverse than modern London, but erased the Jewishness of one character and cast an Arab man as a canonically black character, whose isolation and inability to blend in are actually essential.

Sorry, rant over. I just think the hubris required to refuse to read the source material is actually common in the entertainment industry. Look at Star Wars or the Rings of Power. These people are paid millions of dollars to reproduce books or other stories and instead of immersing themselves in the original material, they think, “nah, I know better.”

That show is already pretty well written but if Dunk and Egg weren't cast perfectly and didn't have amazing chemistry it would fall apart like The Last of Us show did because that was poorly cast.
